ctx.name: Taya
ctx.notes: Shes being doing great with the new university class

introQueue.label: The real version
introQueue.name: Tell me about your week — the real version.
introQueue.description: Kim Scott's 'care personally' made concrete. Signals that a performed answer won't cut it.

introQueue.label: Agenda check
introQueue.name: Before we get into it, anything you want to make sure we cover today?
introQueue.description: Semi-set early question. Gives the report explicit permission to set the agenda before the manager's plan takes over.

introQueue.label: Self-read opener
introQueue.name: Before I share my view, how do you think the last stretch has gone?
introQueue.description: Anchors the conversation in their self-assessment first; gap vs manager's view becomes a signal.

introQueue.label: What they're proud of
introQueue.name: What's something from the last quarter you're genuinely proud of — not what looks good, what actually was good?
introQueue.description: Separates real wins from performative ones; calibrates their sense of impact.

focus_points.label: Impact in the new university class
focus_points.reason: What she’s driving through the new university class—outcomes, leverage, and what changed because of her.

focus_points.label: How her coaching feedback lands
focus_points.reason: How her coaching conversations are landing with learners and peers—clarity, specificity, and whether people act on what she says.

focus_points.label: Feedback she’s getting and asking for
focus_points.reason: Whether she’s receiving useful feedback from learners, other coaches, and leadership—and whether she’s asking for the next layer of coaching input.

brief.coreIssue: This performance conversation is likely about how Taya’s senior-level coaching is translating into visible impact in the new university class.
brief.openingQuestion: What changes have you seen in the new university class that make you feel your coaching is really moving people forward?
listenFor: whether she names a specific learner, cohort moment, or class shift instead of speaking only in general positives
whether she volunteers how learners or peer coaches responded to her feedback and what they did next
if they mention where she is still testing her approach or wants sharper input on how her coaching lands
avoid: do not turn a positive performance discussion into a vague praise session with no evidence or examples
do not jump straight into improvement points before grounding the conversation in what senior-level impact she is already creating
brief.goodOutcome: You and Taya have agreed what senior Acting Coach impact looks like in this university class and one concrete example of coaching influence she should keep building on.
brief.suggestedAction: Before the 1:1, pick one specific example from the new university class where her coaching changed learner behaviour, class outcomes, or peer coach practice.

issues: listenFor may lack observable behavioural cue: "if they mention where she is still testing her approach or w…"

briefing.headline: Taya gave mostly incomplete answers with a partial read signal, but the only clear thread is she wants to reduce marking effort in the new university class.
summary_bullets: Taya repeatedly avoided the direct “what changed / what caused it” specifics and answered with fragments like “i never said that,” leaving the real coaching mechanism unclear.
The one consistent operational need is marking time: she said “it took sl long” and “i want to have a way to do less marking,” which also ties to her low clarity score.
Learner impact is stated at a high level (“They are more confident”) but evidence is thin and indirect (“they didnt wait to be asked to talk”), so the impact story needs tightening.
briefing.understanding_paragraph: We learned that Taya is confident about the learner outcome in the new university class, but she can’t (or won’t) articulate the specific coaching behaviors that produced it. We also learned she feels the workload friction is in marking and is actively searching for a way to reduce it. The session didn’t clarify what feedback she’s getting beyond “they loved the class and sad it was over,” so the feedback loop is not well-defined yet.

axes.meaning: Weak signal on wellbeing; not enough detail to judge sustainability or stress.
Slightly low signal: she focuses on reducing effort (“do less marking”) more than proposing new initiatives.
Clarity is low: she can’t connect coaching actions to outcomes and repeats non-answers (“i never said that”).
A real positive pattern: she can identify learner confidence evidence and sets a concrete change goal (less marking).

briefing.brutal_truth_employee: Taya keeps dodging the core causal questions with “i never said that” and “i never said that,” even when asked what she did. She does name the real constraint: “it took sl long” and “i want to have a way to do less marking,” so the work friction is the driving issue.
briefing.brutal_truth_manager: When you asked for specifics (“What specifically made it slow?” and “What did you do that caused that?”), Taya answered with fragments and then blocked causality with “i never said that.” That was the moment to force one concrete example: one learner moment, one coaching action, and one measurable change—otherwise the conversation stays at the “nice to complete the course” level.

next_actions.action: Schedule a 30-minute follow-up with Taya to map one marking workflow end-to-end and list 3 concrete steps to cut time.
Confirm with Taya the exact learner confidence evidence: name 2 observable behaviors and how she will capture them next run.
Re-run the feedback question with a forced choice: “Who gives you feedback (name 1-2 sources) and what decision did it change?”

watch_for: Before next 1:1: if Taya again answers “i never said that” to causal questions, treat clarity as unresolved, not just a communication style.
Within two weeks: if she can produce a concrete marking-time reduction plan (steps + owner + expected minutes saved), growth is real; if not, the goal stays vague.

question.label: The real version
question.name: Tell me about your week — the real version.
question.description: Kim Scott's 'care personally' made concrete. Signals that a performed answer won't cut it.

transcript.answer: Yeah she saiid it was nice to complete the course,
transcript.note: Pivot/off-topic answer gives no clear signal on wellbeing or engagement; likely references someone else's reaction to completing the course.

question.label: Self-read opener
question.name: Before I share my view, how do you think the last stretch has gone?
question.description: Anchors the conversation in their self-assessment first; gap vs manager's view becomes a signal.

transcript.answer: i want to now how i can do the marking easier, it took sl long
transcript.note: Pivoted from self-assessment to a specific friction point — marking feels inefficient and time-heavy, signaling low clarity on how to do the work effectively and mild strain on engagement.

question.label: Marking friction
question.name: Marking took so long — what specifically made it slow?
question.description: Drills into the concrete marking friction she raised so the conversation stays anchored in her actual work.

transcript.answer: there was a marked improvement in the sutdents
transcript.note: Pivot/off-topic: she answered with learner improvement rather than what made marking slow; this also volunteers class impact evidence worth using next.

question.label: Class impact evidence
question.name: What changed for learners because of your work on the new university class?
question.description: Surfaces concrete outcomes and leverage from her work in the class.

transcript.note: [SHALLOW] 'They are more confident' names a vague outcome with no concrete evidence of what changed.

question.label: Confidence evidence
question.name: You said 'more confident' — what did they do differently that showed that?
question.description: Clarifies the vague outcome by asking for observable learner behaviour as evidence.

transcript.answer: they didnt wait to be asked to talk , theyu just go up and went for it
transcript.note: She gave concrete behavioural evidence of learner confidence and initiative, indicating strong learner growth and a mild positive engagement signal.

question.label: Student initiative
question.name: Those moments where they 'just went for it' — what did you do that caused that?
question.description: Pins the observed learner behaviour back to her specific coaching actions before moving on.

transcript.answer: I never said it was taking more effort
transcript.note: [SHALLOW] 'I never said it was taking more effort' does not answer what she did to cause the student initiative; [BUDGET-STARVED]

question.label: Hidden friction
question.name: Where is the coaching work still taking more effort than it should?
question.description: Finds the specific friction points that are easy to miss when the overall picture looks strong.

transcript.note: [SHALLOW] 'i never said that' disputes the premise but does not name where effort is high or low; [THREAD-DEFERRED-WINDDOWN] denied premise of hidden friction, but wind-down requires advancing to cause/commit.

question.label: Input quality
question.name: What feedback are you not getting enough of right now?
question.description: Surfaces gaps in the feedback loop from learners, peers, or leadership.

transcript.answer: they loved the class and sad it was over
transcript.note: Pivot/off-topic answer praised learner reaction rather than naming missing feedback; final turn so queue advances to the closer.

question.label: Close the loop
question.name: Given what we talked through, what is the first concrete thing you want to change before we meet again?
question.description: Ends on a specific commitment with a near-term action.

transcript.answer: i want to have a way to do less marking
transcript.note: She names a concrete improvement target — reducing marking load — which shows some next-step clarity and a modest change orientation.

closer.label: Close the loop
closer.name: Given what we talked through, what is the first concrete thing you want to change before we meet again?
closer.description: Ends on a specific commitment with a near-term action.
